L0_description: The narrative explicitly describes strong, sustained winds with horizontal flags that were observable at the exit point. Two companions decided conditions were too dangerous and walked down. The jumper was warned but chose to jump anyway, driven by pressure to get a final jump before leaving. The decision to jump in clearly identifiable unsafe wind conditions constitutes a human error — the environmental conditions were not unforeseeable but were knowingly accepted.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper decided to jump in strong, sustained wind conditions that were observable and worsening, after companions declined to jump and explicitly warned him. This decision alone was sufficient to produce the fatal outcome: the wind overpowered his canopy's forward penetration capability regardless of execution quality, pushing him back into the wall. The canopy opened on-heading and was fully inflated — execution was adequate, but the conditions made survival impossible.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: Brett was on his last day in Lauterbrunnen and described as 'desperate' to get one final jump before his flight home the next morning. This time pressure and desire to complete a final jump drove his decision to proceed despite clearly dangerous wind conditions, explicit warnings from companions, and two partners declining to jump.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate site assessment for hazards
L2_preconditions.description: Despite observing sustained strong winds with horizontal flags at the restaurant and conditions worsening over a 45-minute wait, the jumper failed to adequately assess the wind's effect on canopy penetration capability at this specific cliff site. Accepted practice requires evaluating wind at opening altitude and recognizing that wind overpowering canopy forward speed makes the jump unsurvivable regardless of execution quality.

L0_description: The jumper misrouted the bridle around the right leg strap during packing, a rigging error that prevented canopy extraction and caused a total malfunction (PC in tow). Additionally, neither the jumper nor his partner performed a gear check before exit, which would have caught the misrouting. Rushed packing the evening before and failure to conduct a pre-jump equipment inspection are clear human errors.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Memory failure - omitted gear check
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Neither the jumper nor his partner performed a pre-exit gear check that would have detected the misrouted bridle. The narrative explicitly states that running a hand along the bridle routing would have immediately caught the error. This omission allowed the rigging error to persist to exit.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error - bridle misrouted during packing
L1_unsafe_acts.description: During packing, the jumper routed the bridle around the right leg strap instead of properly through the container system. This rigging error meant the fully inflated pilot chute could not extract the canopy from the container, producing a total malfunction (PC in tow) and closed-container impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to perform a pre-jump gear check
L2_preconditions.description: Neither the jumper nor his partner performed a pre-exit gear check. The narrative explicitly states that running a hand along the bridle routing would have immediately detected the misrouted bridle, preventing the total malfunction. This omission directly enabled the bridle misrouting skill-based error to persist undetected to exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper 'seemed in a rush because he wanted to go to sleep early to catch the early morning conditions.' This haste during packing contributed to the bridle misrouting error by creating conditions where careful attention to rigging was compromised by the desire to finish quickly.

L2_preconditions.label: Inattention/Complacency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper packed in a rushed manner and neither he nor his partner checked the gear at the exit point. The failure to maintain sufficient attention during packing allowed the bridle to be routed around the leg strap unnoticed, and the absence of a gear check at the exit reflected reduced alertness to the possibility of rigging errors.

L0_description: The jumper chose to fly a wingsuit proximity line in flat-light conditions (snow, no shadows, no contrast, no texture) that eliminated depth perception cues, leading to fatal loss of altitude awareness and ground impact without deployment initiation. Flat-light conditions were observable and assessable before the jump — heavy fresh snow with low-level clouds creating zero contrast were identifiable hazards that a competent BASE jumper could reasonably have recognized as dangerous for proximity flying. The decision to jump and fly near terrain in these conditions constitutes a human error in judgment.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Visual illusion from flat light over snow
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper flew into featureless, contrast-free snow terrain in flat light conditions that eliminated all depth cues. He perceived himself to be at a safe altitude when he was actually at ground level. His relaxed body posture and complete absence of deployment initiation or corrective input confirm he acted consistently with a false perception of altitude. The visual illusion produced by flat light over fresh snow caused him to fly into terrain at terminal speed without any awareness of proximity.

L2_preconditions.label: Spatial Disorientation
L2_preconditions.description: The flat light conditions over featureless fresh snow eliminated all visual depth cues, causing the jumper's sensory systems to fail to correctly perceive his altitude and proximity to terrain. This spatial disorientation directly produced the Layer 1 perceptual error in which he believed he had plenty of altitude when he was at ground level.

L2_preconditions.label: Flat light over featureless snow terrain
L2_preconditions.description: Heavy fresh snow with no shadows, no contrast, and no texture created complete flat light conditions that eliminated all visual depth cues. This environmental condition directly degraded the jumper's ability to perceive altitude during flight, producing the visual illusion that caused the Layer 1 perceptual error. The condition was present and observable before the jump but actively impaired performance during execution.

L0_description: The narrative provides positive evidence that the fatality resulted from a manufacturing defect in the harness webbing (incorrect heat treatment during production causing internal degradation undetectable through normal inspection). The jumper's packing, rigging, maintenance, equipment selection, and execution were all confirmed correct. The equipment failure was independent of any human error, constituting an unforeseeable material failure.

The narrative provides positive evidence of an unforeseeable environmental event: a massive localized rotor caused by a temperature inversion and sudden thermal event that violently collapsed the canopy post-opening. Meteorologists confirmed the event could not have been predicted or detected from the exit point, and no wind indicators showed movement before or after the jump. The jumper's exit, tracking, deployment, and packing were all within accepted practice, leaving no human error pathway.

The jumper suffered a massive brainstem stroke caused by a congenital arterial malformation that had never produced symptoms, resulting in instant incapacitation and inability to deploy his parachute. He had no prior medical history or diagnosis, his recent physical showed no cardiovascular issues, and the condition was not reasonably foreseeable. This constitutes a sudden medical event independent of human error.

The narrative explicitly identifies two human errors: the jumper chose to exit in wind conditions (20 knots sustained, gusts over 30) that four experienced companions had deemed unsafe and stood down from, and he then executed an unstable exit without prioritizing deployment. The strong crosswind was observable and forecastable—not an unforeseeable event—making the decision to jump a human error rather than an other cause.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper decided to jump in sustained 20-knot crosswinds with gusts exceeding 30 knots, conditions that four experienced companions had unanimously deemed unsafe and stood down from. This decision alone was sufficient to produce the fatal outcome: the strong crosswind gust caught him immediately on exit, pushed him back toward the wall, and made recovery impossible regardless of execution quality.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After exiting into the crosswind, the jumper went from stable to head-low within the first second and tumbled along the face for three to four seconds without ever getting a hand to his pilot chute. Given the hostile conditions, prioritizing immediate deployment over stability recovery was the correct action, but he failed to execute any deployment attempt before striking the ledge approximately 80 meters below the exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: Four experienced companions unanimously assessed conditions as unsafe and stood down, yet the jumper chose to remain and ultimately jump. This indicates the jumper unreasonably overestimated his own capability to handle conditions that peers with equal or greater experience rejected, directly contributing to the decision to jump in dangerous winds.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient experience for conditions
L2_preconditions.description: With 90 BASE jumps and 3 seasons, the jumper lacked the depth of experience needed to manage an exit in extreme crosswind conditions. His inability to transition from a destabilized exit to any deployment attempt—failing to prioritize pulling over stability recovery—suggests insufficient skill development for the emergency response demanded by these conditions.

L2_preconditions.label: Strong crosswind gust on exit
L2_preconditions.description: A strong crosswind gust caught the jumper immediately upon exit, pushing him sideways and back toward the wall and driving him from stable to head-low within the first second. This environmental force directly degraded his ability to maintain body control and execute any deployment, contributing to the technique failure. The wind conditions were foreseeable (and were the basis of the 101D decision error), but their physical effect on execution is what degraded the 102S performance.

L0_description: Multiple human errors contributed to this fatality. The jumper chose to jump while severely impaired from heavy alcohol consumption the night before (vomiting, unsteady, splitting headache), which degraded his physical coordination and led to an unstable exit. He then failed to deploy his pilot chute during approximately 6 seconds of freefall, fixating on stability recovery rather than prioritizing deployment—violating the fundamental principle that deploying unstable is preferable to impact with nothing out. Peers warned him not to jump, and he disregarded their advice.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Failure to deploy parachute
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper fixated on recovering stability during freefall rather than deploying the pilot chute. Despite approximately 6 seconds of freefall, the PC remained in the BOC at impact. Deploying the parachute even from an unstable position would have produced a canopy overhead; the omission of deployment was the direct mechanism of the fatal ground impact.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error on exit
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper's legs buckled on the push-off, producing an immediately unstable exit with a roll onto his back and slow tumble. The intended action was a stable exit, but physical execution failed, likely degraded by his impaired physical state. This unstable exit consumed the freefall time and created the conditions requiring emergency deployment.

L2_preconditions.label: Substance Effects
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was heavily intoxicated the night before and still visibly impaired at jump time — splitting headache, vomiting twice on the hike, visibly unsteady. This hangover state directly degraded his physical coordination, causing his legs to buckle on the exit push-off and producing the unstable exit.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: During freefall the jumper fixated entirely on recovering a stable body position rather than deploying the pilot chute. He channelized his attention on stability recovery to the exclusion of the higher-priority task of deployment, spending all 6 seconds of available freefall without reaching for the BOC.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative states the jumper had jumped hung over before without issues and that the decision to jump impaired was not unusual for him. This indicates a sustained pattern of jumping while physically compromised — normalization of deviance — which he never corrected despite the obvious risk, enabling the impaired state that degraded his exit performance on this jump.

L0_description: Multiple human errors contributed to the fatality. The jumper used a tracking suit on a cliff with only two skydives in it and zero cliff jumps with it, far below the accepted minimum of 40-50 tracking suit skydives before BASE use. He chose an intermediate-to-advanced exit requiring effective sub-terminal tracking despite having no tracking suit cliff experience, rejected advice to start with a more forgiving exit, and jumped while visibly exhausted after driving 14 hours through the night, degrading physical and cognitive performance. These factors—insufficient tracking suit training, inadequate progression, ignoring mentor guidance, and fatigue—combined to produce the fatal inability to achieve forward separation.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper exited and attempted to track away from the cliff but failed to achieve an effective tracking position in the suit. Despite understanding the terrain profile and executing a decent push, he could not generate forward drive and fell at a steep angle, impacting the ledge at approximately 7 seconds. The suit was 'flying him' rather than the other way around, indicating inadequate physical mastery of the tracking suit in flight, which directly produced insufficient separation and fatal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient tracking suit training
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only two skydives in the tracking suit and had never jumped it from a cliff. Accepted practice requires 40-50 tracking suit skydives before BASE use, and mastery of the suit's body position for maximum forward speed and glide. His two skydives were grossly insufficient to develop the skill needed to fly the suit effectively on a cliff exit requiring sub-terminal tracking.

L2_preconditions.label: Fatigue
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper drove 14 hours straight through the night and was visibly exhausted at the exit point. This physical fatigue from sleep deprivation would have degraded his coordination, reaction time, and ability to execute the demanding body position required for effective tracking suit flight, directly contributing to his inability to achieve forward drive.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper rejected a suggestion to start with a more forgiving exit, stating the chosen exit 'looked easy from the footage.' He overestimated his ability to fly a tracking suit he had barely used, basing his confidence on watching videos rather than on actual demonstrated competence. This unreasonable overestimation of capability contributed to his proceeding with a task that exceeded his skill level.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate site assessment for hazards
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper chose an intermediate-to-advanced exit with a technical section where the wall comes back in, requiring effective tracking suit performance he had not demonstrated. While he understood the terrain profile intellectually, he failed to adequately assess whether his near-zero tracking suit experience was sufficient for an exit demanding proficient suit flight to clear the technical section.

L0_description: The jumper experienced a 180° off-heading on a static line jump from a building and attempted correction by grabbing only the right toggle and pulling it hard, which drove her into a diving turn into the building. The domain reference establishes that the correct response to a 180° off-heading near an object is to use rear risers (both risers to halt forward speed, then release one), not a single hard toggle input which produces a diving turn toward the object. This incorrect off-heading correction technique constitutes a human error in skill-based performance.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After experiencing a 180° off-heading opening facing the building, the jumper grabbed only the right toggle and pulled it hard, initiating a diving turn into the building. The accepted technique for a 180° off-heading is to use rear risers — applying both rear risers to halt forward speed, then releasing one to turn — or to grab both toggles simultaneously and apply asymmetric input. Pulling a single toggle hard from a 180° off-heading drove the canopy into the building rather than arresting forward movement and turning away. The execution of the heading correction was the direct mechanism that produced the fatal object strike.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient experience for building jumps
L2_preconditions.description: With 90 BASE jumps and 3 seasons, the jumper's experience level was relatively low for building jumps, which require refined object avoidance skills and instant, correct heading correction technique. The accepted progression path emphasizes practicing off-heading corrections extensively at simple spans before progressing to objects where off-headings are life-threatening. The incorrect single-toggle technique under pressure suggests insufficient training in emergency heading corrections, a skill that must be drilled to the point of automatic muscle memory.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: Facing a 180° off-heading directly into a glass facade at very low altitude from a static line deployment, the jumper's response — grabbing only one toggle and pulling it hard — is consistent with a startle-driven, reflexive reaction rather than a deliberate corrective technique. The sudden, high-threat situation likely triggered an automatic response that bypassed the correct procedure (rear risers or dual-toggle input), contributing to the flawed execution.

L0_description: The jumper chose to exit in near-zero visibility conditions (fog obscuring the wall, fjord, and landing area) when all other jumpers decided not to jump. This decision to jump in conditions where terrain hazards were not visible constitutes a clear human error in judgment. The fog was observable and present at the exit point before the jump, making this a foreseeable environmental hazard the jumper chose to accept rather than an unforeseeable event. The jumper impacted a protruding ledge he could not see, with his pilot chute still stowed, indicating he never had the opportunity to deploy due to loss of altitude awareness and terrain visibility.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper decided to exit in near-zero visibility conditions where the wall, fjord, and landing area were not visible, relying solely on his sense of heading rather than visual reference. This decision alone was sufficient to produce the fatal outcome regardless of execution quality, as the fog made it impossible to see and avoid terrain obstacles such as the protruding ledge he struck. All other jumpers recognized the conditions as unjumpable and chose to hike down.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper explicitly stated he had done the exit dozens of times and knew the heading by feel, dismissing the need for visual reference that all other jumpers recognized as essential. This unreasonable overestimation of his ability to navigate blind through complex terrain directly drove his decision to exit in near-zero visibility conditions.

L2_preconditions.label: Near-zero visibility from fog/clouds
L2_preconditions.description: While the decision to jump in fog is coded at L1, the fog itself actively degraded the jumper's performance during execution by eliminating all visual reference to terrain. He could not see the protruding ledge he struck, making it impossible to detect and avoid the obstacle during his track regardless of his heading accuracy.

L0_description: The jumper interrupted his track to adjust a helmet-mounted GoPro during freefall, causing loss of tracking body position and separation from the wall. This distraction from a core task (maintaining track and object separation) led to uncorrected drift into the cliff and impact without deployment. Adjusting a camera mid-flight constitutes a human error, and the domain reference explicitly identifies video as a risk factor and distraction.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper decided to reach up and adjust his helmet-mounted GoPro during an active tracking flight near terrain. This decision to divert attention and a hand from tracking to camera adjustment was inappropriate for the situation, as it directly caused loss of tracking body position and object separation.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attention failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: After his body position degraded from the camera adjustment, the jumper failed to recognize or respond to his leftward drift toward the wall and his decreasing separation from terrain. The ledge was in clear view for several seconds, yet he made no corrective input and never deployed his parachute, indicating a complete loss of situational awareness regarding his proximity to the object.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's attention became channelized on the camera adjustment task, excluding awareness of his spatial position relative to the wall. This fixation on the camera prevented him from monitoring his drift and proximity to the ledge, directly contributing to his failure to recognize the threat and take corrective action or deploy.

L2_preconditions.label: Pressure/Haste/Misplaced Motivation
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper's role as the designated filmer on this two-way created a motivation to ensure camera functionality during the flight. This misplaced prioritization of filming over flight safety contributed to his decision to reach up and adjust the GoPro mid-track rather than maintaining tracking body position and object separation.

L0_description: The jumper had a known, recurring problem with angle of attack control that caused repeated near-miss stalls. He explicitly acknowledged the need for additional skydiving training to address this deficiency but continued jumping cliffs without completing that training. His decision to continue proximity flying despite recognized skill deficiencies constitutes a clear human error, and the fatal stall and terrain impact were a direct consequence of this unaddressed problem.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper knowingly continued proximity wingsuit BASE jumping despite recognizing that his angle of attack control was inadequate, having experienced at least four near-miss stalls in the preceding months. He identified the need for dedicated skydiving training to address the deficiency but repeatedly chose to continue cliff flying without completing that training. This decision to keep jumping with a known, serious skill deficit was the producing mechanism: it placed him in a flight regime where the recurring stall pattern manifested without sufficient altitude for recovery.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error
L1_unsafe_acts.description: During the flight, approximately 15 seconds after exit, the jumper allowed his angle of attack to become too high, entering a stall that converted his glide path from shallow to nearly vertical. This was the same execution failure pattern he had experienced on at least four prior occasions. The stall eliminated his available lift and he was unable to recover before impacting terrain, producing the fatal outcome.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper explicitly acknowledged that his angle of attack control was inadequate and stated he needed to return to skydiving for dedicated stall recovery training. Despite experiencing at least four near-miss stalls over three months, he never undertook the corrective training, normalizing the recurring problem and continuing to jump each weekend. This sustained pattern of inaction enabled the decision to keep flying proximity with a known serious deficiency.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient AoA control skill for proximity flying
L2_preconditions.description: With approximately 80 wingsuit BASE jumps over two seasons and a documented pattern of repeated near-stall events, the jumper's angle of attack management skill was insufficient for the demands of proximity wingsuit terrain flying. His baseline capability in stall avoidance and recovery was inadequate for the task, directly contributing to the in-flight stall that produced the fatal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper knew from repeated near-miss stalls that his AoA control technique was deficient and identified a specific corrective action (50 skydives practicing stall recovery) but never executed it. This sustained failure to address the known skill gap meant the same execution error pattern persisted uncorrected, directly enabling the technique failure on the fatal flight.

L0_description: The jumper experienced a 90-degree off-heading opening toward guy wires and failed to make any corrective input (no toggle or riser action), freezing with arms at sides despite approximately two seconds of available reaction time. This failure to execute heading correction constitutes a human error. Additionally, jumping a 900-foot antenna at night with only 60 BASE jumps, only two prior antenna jumps (both from taller/more forgiving structures), and limited experience with the restricted heading range inherent to guyed antennas indicates the jumper was operating beyond their experience level.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attention failure / freeze response
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper's canopy opened with a 90-degree off-heading pointing directly at guy wires approximately 40 feet away. Despite having approximately two seconds to initiate a heading correction via rear risers or brake input, the jumper made no control input whatsoever — his arms remained at his sides. Multiple witnesses confirmed he appeared to completely freeze. The failure to execute any heading correction, a skill that should be reflexive for a BASE jumper, was the direct mechanism that produced the fatal guy-wire strike.

L2_preconditions.label: Startle Response/Freeze
L2_preconditions.description: Multiple witnesses confirmed the jumper appeared to completely freeze upon seeing the guy wires directly in front of him after the off-heading opening. This automatic physiological freeze response rendered him temporarily unable to initiate any corrective action during the approximately two seconds available before impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient antenna experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 60 BASE jumps and just two prior antenna jumps, both from taller structures with guy wires further from the exit point. This limited experience with the specific hazard profile of antennas — particularly the proximity of guy wires and the narrow heading tolerance — likely contributed to his inability to execute a reflexive heading correction under pressure. Insufficient practice of off-heading corrections in guy-wire environments meant the required response was not yet automatic muscle memory.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate site assessment for hazards
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper selected an 800-foot exit on a 900-foot antenna where guy wires were approximately 40 feet from the exit point — significantly closer than on the taller structures he had previously jumped. With only two prior antenna jumps from more forgiving configurations, jumping this antenna at night with closer guy wires represented an inadequate assessment of how the site's hazard profile matched his experience level. The proximity of the wires left virtually no margin for the off-heading that occurred.

L2_preconditions.label: Night conditions impaired visual response
L2_preconditions.description: The jump was conducted at 23:30 at night, which degraded the jumper's ability to visually acquire the guy wires and orient himself immediately after opening. Reduced visual acuity in darkness likely delayed recognition of the threat and compounded the freeze response, further reducing the already minimal time available for corrective action.

L0_description: The jumper knowingly used a pilot chute with a large tear in the mesh after being advised by companions to replace it and offered spare equipment. This decision to jump with visibly damaged equipment constitutes a human error in equipment maintenance and pre-jump inspection, directly causing the PC's inability to inflate and the resulting total malfunction (PC in tow / closed-container impact).

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper decided to jump with a pilot chute that had a massive tear in the mesh, despite being advised it needed replacement and being offered spare PCs by other jumpers. He examined the damage and consciously chose to use the compromised PC, judging it would 'still catch enough air.' This decision directly produced the total malfunction: the damaged PC could not inflate, fluttered and collapsed in his burble, resulting in a closed-container impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper examined the torn PC, dismissed warnings from multiple experienced peers, and rejected offers of spare equipment, stating it would 'still catch enough air.' This demonstrates an unreasonable overestimation of the damaged equipment's capability and his own judgment, directly enabling the decision to jump with a critically compromised pilot chute.

L0_description: The narrative explicitly describes multiple human errors contributing to the fatality: the group failed to establish a proper separation plan, exit order delays, flight paths, or opening altitudes for the multi-way jump. David tracked over the second jumper and deployed directly into the second jumper's inflating canopy without checking his airspace, resulting in canopy entanglement. These failures in multi-way jump planning and airspace awareness are clear departures from accepted practice.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Attention failure
L1_unsafe_acts.description: David failed to check his airspace before deploying his pilot chute, despite tracking directly over the second jumper. He did not monitor the position of the jumper below him, which was critical information available during execution. This failure to clear airspace resulted in deploying directly into the second jumper's inflating canopy, causing fatal canopy entanglement.

L2_preconditions.label: Channelized Attention/Fixation
L2_preconditions.description: David's attention during tracking was likely channelized on his own track and deployment timing rather than maintaining awareness of the jumpers below him. This failure to scan for other jumpers before deploying indicates attention was narrowly focused, excluding the critical cue of the second jumper's position directly beneath his flight path.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate briefing or coordination in group jumps
L2_preconditions.description: The narrative explicitly states nobody properly discussed exit order, separation plan, specific delays, flight paths, or opening altitudes. This lack of coordinated planning for the 3-way jump meant David had no pre-established framework for maintaining separation or clearing airspace, directly contributing to his failure to check for the second jumper before deploying.

L0_description: The jumper deliberately delayed pilot chute extraction too long, resulting in insufficient altitude for canopy inflation before impact. The narrative explicitly states he had a pattern of progressively pushing delays longer despite warnings from other jumpers, and on this jump he 'simply pushed it one second too far.' This constitutes a clear human error in deployment timing.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper deliberately chose to delay deployment far beyond safe limits, pulling very low just above the water from a span object. He had a pattern of progressively extending delays from 4 to 6 to 8+ seconds despite warnings from other jumpers. On this jump he intentionally pushed the delay one second too far, leaving insufficient altitude for canopy extraction and inflation before impact. The decision to pull at that altitude was itself the producing mechanism of the fatality.

L2_preconditions.label: Overconfidence
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper progressively extended delays from 4 to 6 to 8+ seconds over the season, demonstrating an unreasonable overestimation of his ability to judge minimum safe pull altitude. Multiple other jumpers warned him about his increasingly low pulls, but he dismissed or disregarded their concerns, indicating he believed he could safely manage delays that others recognized as dangerous.

L2_preconditions.label: Failure to Correct Known Deficiency
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper was aware through direct feedback from multiple other jumpers that his progressively longer delays were dangerous, yet he failed to take corrective action. This was a sustained pattern of inaction over the season despite repeated warnings, representing normalization of deviance as he gradually accepted increasingly risky delay times as his personal standard.

L0_description: The jumper flew an expert-level wingsuit (Aura 1) with only approximately 10 skydives in it before using it for BASE, far below the accepted minimum of 40-60 skydives in the specific suit. This insufficient experience with the suit's flight characteristics led to an in-flight stall from which he could not recover, resulting in terminal impact. This represents a clear progression error in equipment selection and inadequate preparation with the specific wingsuit.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error: stall and failure to recover
L1_unsafe_acts.description: Mid-flight, the jumper's angle of attack shifted to a stall condition, causing his glide to transition from shallow to nearly vertical. He was unable to recover from the stall and continued sinking until terminal impact with terrain. The intended course of action (flying the intermediate line) was appropriate, but execution failed when he allowed the suit to enter a stall regime and lacked the technique to recover, consistent with insufficient experience on the expert-level wingsuit.

L2_preconditions.label: Insufficient experience on expert wingsuit
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only approximately 10 skydives in the Aura 1 expert wingsuit before taking it off a cliff. Accepted practice requires 40-60 wingsuit skydives with the specific suit before wingsuit BASE jumping. With insufficient flights to understand the stall boundaries of the expert-level suit, he lacked the skill and muscle memory needed to manage angle of attack and recover from a stall in that suit.

L2_preconditions.label: Premature upgrade to expert wingsuit
L2_preconditions.description: With only 40 wingsuit BASE jumps on intermediate suits and 200 wingsuit skydives total, the jumper upgraded to an expert-level wingsuit before fully utilizing his intermediate suit's performance. The domain reference explicitly identifies upgrading wingsuits before fully utilizing current suit performance as a recognized error. His overall wingsuit BASE experience was insufficient for the demands of an expert suit in proximity terrain.

L2_preconditions.label: Inadequate equipment configuration for planned jump
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper selected an expert-level wingsuit (Aura 1) for a proximity terrain flight despite having only approximately 10 skydives in it. Accepted practice requires extensive familiarity with a specific suit in skydiving before BASE use. This equipment selection decision created the conditions under which the stall and failure to recover occurred.

L2_preconditions.label: Thermal activity during flight
L2_preconditions.description: The weather was described as sunny and hot with thermals producing. Thermal activity can create unpredictable vertical air movement along the flight path, potentially contributing to the mid-flight angle of attack shift and stall. The jumper's limited experience on the expert suit would have made him less able to manage unexpected atmospheric disturbances during flight.

L0_description: The jumper had only 5 skydives and 0 BASE jumps, far below the accepted minimum of 200+ skydives and proper BASE progression before attempting any BASE jump, let alone a slider-up cliff jump with a tracking suit. This represents a severe violation of accepted progression norms ('death camper' level). Additionally, the narrative indicates possible unstable exit, insufficient forward separation, and excessive delay at a site known to require precise timing, all pointing to human error in judgment, skill, and execution.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Poor judgment or risk acceptance
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The jumper attempted a slider-up tracking suit cliff jump with only 5 skydives and zero BASE jumps, representing a catastrophic departure from accepted progression norms (minimum 200+ skydives and BASE experience before cliff jumping, plus 40-50 tracking suit skydives before BASE use with a tracking suit). This decision alone was sufficient to produce the fatal outcome, as the jumper lacked the fundamental skills (exit technique, sub-terminal tracking, deployment timing, canopy control) required to survive any slider-up cliff jump.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Technique error on exit and/or delay management
L1_unsafe_acts.description: The narrative indicates the jumper likely had an unstable exit and failed to achieve adequate forward separation from the cliff, consistent with the lack of any freefall or tracking training. The jumper either impacted the cliff in freefall after taking too long a delay, or opened too close to the wall with an off-heading. The execution failure in exit stability, tracking, and/or deployment timing was the direct mechanism producing the fatal impact.

L2_preconditions.label: Grossly insufficient training and experience
L2_preconditions.description: The jumper had only 5 skydives total, zero BASE jumps, and zero wingsuit or tracking suit skydives. This is catastrophically below accepted minimums (200+ skydives, 40-50 tracking suit skydives, BASE experience before cliff jumping). This complete lack of training and experience meant the jumper had no basis for understanding the risks involved, directly enabling the decision to attempt the jump.

L2_preconditions.label: No freefall or tracking skill base
L2_preconditions.description: With only 5 skydives and no tracking suit experience, the jumper had never developed the body position skills, sub-terminal tracking ability, exit technique, or deployment timing required for a slider-up cliff jump. This absence of foundational skills directly caused the execution failures in exit stability, tracking, and delay management that produced the fatal impact.

L0_description: The jumper made a critical packing error by placing the pilot chute inside the main container beneath the closing flaps instead of routing it to the BOC pouch. This resulted in a total malfunction (PC in tow / PC trapped in container) when the BOC handle extracted without the PC. Additionally, the jumper chose to jump in deteriorating weather with increasing winds and rain, which constitutes a decision to jump in marginal conditions.

L1_unsafe_acts.label: Memory failure during packing
L1_unsafe_acts.description: During packing, the jumper placed the pilot chute inside the main container on top of the folded canopy instead of routing it out to the BOC pouch. This was a procedural execution error in a known sequence — the jumper knew the PC must be in the BOC but failed to route it correctly. When the BOC handle was pulled on jump, no PC was attached, resulting in a total malfunction and fatal impact.

L0_description: The narrative is entirely absent and no structured fields provide meaningful information about the circumstances of the fatality. There is insufficient information to deduce either a human error pathway or a non-human cause.

The narrative provides minimal information: the jumper impacted talus below the exit with the canopy out, and there were no witnesses. No details are available regarding experience, equipment configuration, packing, weather, body position, or the sequence of events leading to impact. There is insufficient information to deduce a human error pathway or to identify a specific non-human cause.

Two experienced witnesses provide contradictory accounts — one reports a late pull, the other reports a pilot chute in tow / bridle issue with an early pull. The gear was not available for inspection, making it impossible to determine whether the cause was a human error (late deployment) or an equipment malfunction. Neither 001HE nor 002OC can be supported with confidence.

The jumper maintained an active body position throughout the flight but never attempted to deploy, with no equipment malfunction, no medical event, and no toxicological findings to explain the failure to pull. The narrative explicitly states 'Nobody knows why he did not pull.' There is insufficient information to deduce a human error pathway or to identify a positive non-human cause.

The cause of death is impact in terminal freefall during a proximity wingsuit flight, but the narrative contains no technical details about what went wrong. The narrative is a tribute/memorial with no information about the specific failure mode, conditions, decisions, or equipment issues. Without details about the flight path, terrain interaction, or any specific error or environmental event, neither human error nor other cause can be supported.

The jumper was found dead with canopy deployed after jumping alone with no witnesses. All proposed explanations (off-heading, packing issue, thermals) are explicitly described as speculation. No one witnessed the jump and no gear inspection was conducted before removal. There is insufficient information to support either a human error pathway or a non-human cause.

The jumper went missing and was found months later with no witnesses, no known jump date, no weather data, and no information about what occurred during the jump. The narrative explicitly states it is impossible to determine what happened. While the site is known for unpredictable crosswinds, there is insufficient information to determine whether human error or an unforeseeable environmental event caused the fatality.

The narrative provides virtually no information about the jump, the jumper, the equipment, the conditions, or the sequence of events. The body was recovered but no details on gear or cause are available. There is insufficient information to deduce either a human error pathway or a non-human cause.

The jumper deployed too late, resulting in impact at line stretch. Regardless of the debated proximate cause (sun glare misjudging altitude, underestimating tracking suit descent rate, or attention fixation), all proposed explanations involve human error in altitude awareness and deployment timing. The jumper failed to deploy at a survivable altitude, which constitutes a human error in the deployment decision.

The narrative states the cause of death was 'Malfunction, Misrigging' and explicitly rules out manufacturer error ('It was clear that it is not a manufacturer error'). This indicates the equipment failure resulted from the jumper's own rigging or packing errors, constituting human error. The jumper experienced a malfunction he was unable to resolve, resulting in impact after 8 seconds of fighting the risers.
